content.body:
SNR measures **how much louder the desired signal is compared to background noise**. Higher SNR = cleaner signal.

Expressed in **decibels (dB)**. There are two versions depending on whether you are comparing voltages or powers:

$$\text{SNR}_{voltage} = 20 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$$

$$\text{SNR}_{power} = 10 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$$

**Rule:** voltage uses **20**, power uses **10**. The factor of 2 difference exists because power is proportional to voltage squared: $P \propto V^2$, so $\log(V^2) = 2\log(V)$.
Always check: is the ratio a **voltage** ratio or a **power** ratio? Use **20** for voltage, **10** for power. A signal that is 10× louder in voltage is 100× louder in power — both give 20 dB.

content.title: SNR Example 2 — Power Ratio 100×
content.body:
**$P_{signal} = 100 \times P_{noise}$**

$$\text{SNR} = 10 \times \log_{10}(100) = 10 \times 2 = \mathbf{20 \text{ dB}}$$

Note: 10× voltage and 100× power give the **same 20 dB** — because $10^2 = 100$. They describe the same physical situation.

content.title: SNR Example 4 — Reverse Calculation
content.body:
**SNR = 60 dB. What is the voltage ratio?**

$$60 = 20 \times \log_{10}(x)$$
$$\log_{10}(x) = 3$$
$$x = 10^3 = \mathbf{1000}$$

The signal is **1000× louder** in voltage than the noise.

**Quantization Noise (Section 6.1.5)**

When a sample is quantized (rounded to the nearest digital level), the difference between the actual analog value and the rounded value is called **quantization error**.

This error is not real noise — it is simply **rounding error** — but it behaves statistically like noise, so it is treated as noise.

**Maximum quantization error** = $\Delta V / 2$ (half of one step size), where $\Delta V$ is the interval between adjacent quantization levels.

Measures how much louder the signal is compared to the quantization error introduced by the digitization process.

$$\boxed{\text{SQNR} \approx 6.02 \times N \text{ dB}}$$

where $N$ = number of bits per sample.

**Derivation:**
$$\text{SQNR} = 20 \times \log_{10}\left(\frac{2^{N-1}}{0.5}\right) = 20 \times \log_{10}(2^N) = 20 \times N \times \log_{10}(2) \approx 20 \times N \times 0.301 = 6.02 \times N$$

**Key insight:** every additional bit of quantization depth adds approximately **6 dB** of signal quality.
Memorize: **SQNR = 6.02 × N dB**. Each extra bit ≈ 6 dB improvement. This is one of the most-tested formulas in this section.

content.title: SQNR Example 1 — 8-bit (Telephone)
content.body:
$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \times 8 = \mathbf{48.16 \text{ dB}}$$

This is typical **telephone quality** — acceptable for voice but noticeable noise in music.

content.title: SQNR Example 2 — 16-bit (CD)
content.body:
$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \times 16 = \mathbf{96.32 \text{ dB}}$$

**CD quality** — far exceeds the dynamic range of most listening environments.

content.title: SQNR Example 3 — Required Bits for 72 dB
content.body:
**How many bits are needed for SQNR ≥ 72 dB?**

$$N = \frac{72}{6.02} = 11.96$$

Since $N$ must be a whole number, round **up** → need at least $N = \mathbf{12}$ bits.

content.title: SQNR Example 4 — 24-bit (Professional)
content.body:
$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \times 24 = \mathbf{144.48 \text{ dB}}$$

**Professional/studio quality** — far exceeds human hearing's dynamic range (~120 dB).

flashcards.q: SNR formula: when do you use 20 vs 10, and why?
flashcards.a: Use **20** for voltage ratios, **10** for power ratios. The reason: power is proportional to voltage squared ($P \propto V^2$), so $\log(V^2) = 2\log(V)$ — this factor of 2 doubles the coefficient from 10 to 20.

flashcards.q: If the signal voltage is 10× the noise voltage, what is the SNR in dB?
What is quantization noise? Is it real noise?
flashcards.a: Quantization noise is the **rounding error** introduced when a sample is rounded to the nearest digital level. It is **not real noise** — it is pure arithmetic error. However, it behaves statistically like noise, so it is analyzed as noise. Max error = $\Delta V / 2$.

flashcards.q: What does SQNR = 6.02 × N mean practically?
flashcards.a: Every **extra bit of quantization depth** adds approximately **6 dB** of signal quality. So going from 8-bit to 16-bit (8 extra bits) gains 8 × 6 = 48 dB — a massive quality improvement.

flashcards.q: An SNR reading is 60 dB. What is the voltage ratio of signal to noise?
flashcards.a:
$60 = 20 \times \log_{10}(x) \Rightarrow \log_{10}(x) = 3 \Rightarrow x = 10^3 = \mathbf{1000}$

The signal is 1000 times louder in voltage than the noise.

flashcards.q: Why does 10× voltage and 100× power give the same dB value?
flashcards.a: Because power scales as voltage squared: $10^2 = 100$. So $20 \times \log(10) = 20$ dB, and $10 \times \log(100) = 20$ dB. They are the same physical situation expressed differently — **same signal, same ratio, same dB**.

problems.q: The signal power is 1000× the noise power. Calculate the SNR in dB.
An audio system uses 12-bit quantization. Calculate its SQNR.
A professional recording system requires an SQNR of at least 120 dB. What is the minimum number of bits required?
problems.a:
$$N = \frac{120}{6.02} = 19.93$$

Round up → need at least $N = \mathbf{20}$ bits per sample.
problems.hint: Rearrange SQNR = 6.02 × N → N = SQNR / 6.02, then round up

problems.q: An SNR is measured as 80 dB. What is the voltage ratio of signal to noise?
problems.a:
$$80 = 20 \times \log_{10}(x)$$
$$\log_{10}(x) = 4$$
$$x = 10^4 = \mathbf{10{,}000}$$

The signal voltage is **10,000× greater** than the noise voltage.
problems.hint: Solve for x: divide both sides by 20, then raise 10 to that power
